verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: On the death of Eusebius Basil seems to have formed a desire that his friend Gregory should succeed to the vacant Metropolitanate; and so he wrote to him, without mentioning the death of the Archbishop, to come to him at Cæsarea, representing himself as dangerously ill.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: Gregory, deeply grieved at the news, set off at once, but had not proceeded far on his way when he learned that Basil was in his usual health, and that the Bishops of the Province were assembling at Cæsarea for the Election of a Metropolitan.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: He saw through the artifice at once; and thinking that Basil had wished to secure his presence at the Metropolis in order that his influence might bring about his own (Basil’s) Election, he wrote him the following indignant letter.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: Nevertheless both he and his father felt that no one was so well fitted to succeed to the vacant throne; and so Gregory wrote in his father’s name the three letters which we have placed next, addressed respectively to the people of Cæsarea, to the Bishops attending the Synod, and to Eusebius Bishop of Samosata.)

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: Do not be surprized if I say something strange, which has not been said before by anyone.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: I think you have the reputation of being a steady safe and strong-minded man, but also of being more simple than safe in much that you plan and do.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: For that which is free from evil is also in proportion slow to suspect evil, as is shewn by what has just occurred.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: You have summoned me to the Metropolis at the moment when a council has been called for the election of a Bishop, and your pretext is very seemly and plausible.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: You pretend to be very ill, indeed at your last breath, and to long to see me and to bid me a last farewell; I do not know with what object, even what my presence can effect in the matter.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: I started in great grief at what had happened; for what could be of higher value to me than your life, or more distressing than your departure?

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: And I shed a fountain of tears; and I wailed aloud; and I felt myself now for the first time unphilosophically disposed.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: What did I leave unperformed of all that befits a funeral?

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: But as soon as I found that the Bishops were assembling at the City, at once I stopped short in my course; and I wondered first that you had not perceived what was proper, or guarded against people’s tongues, which are so given to slander the guileless; and secondly that you did not think the same course to be fitting for me as for yourself, though our life and our rule and everything is common to us both, who have been so closely associated by God from the first.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: Thirdly, for I must say this also, I wondered whether you remembered that such nominations are worthy of the more religious, not of the more powerful, nor of those most in favour with the multitude.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: For these reasons then I backed water, and held back.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: Now, if you think as I do, come to this determination, to avoid these public turmoils and evil suspicions.

verses.book: Correspondence with Saint Basil the Great, Archbishop of Cæsarea.
verses.text: I shall see your Reverence when the matters are settled and time allows, and I shall have more and graver reproaches to address to you.
